coredata.dc:title: Application of cow milk-derived carbon dots/Ag NPs composite as the antibacterial agent

coredata.dc:description: Cow milk-derived carbon dots (CMCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of cow milk, and the as-prepared CMCDs were further extracted by ethyl acetate to obtain amphiphilic CMCDs (ACMCDs). Using the ACMCDs both as a reducing agent and a template, the ACMCDs-supported silver nanoparticles (ACMCD-Ag nanocomposites) were prepared, which showed good biocidal effect on both Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) bacterias. After that, a novel ACMCD-Ag/polymethylmethacrylate nanocomposite antibacterial film was fabricated by solvent casting method. Due to the excellent antibacterial, light admitting, and flexible properties, the nanocomposite antibacterial film is considered to be of great potential in applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cow milk
Nanocomposite
Extraction
Antibacterial thin film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the ACMCD-Ag/PMMA antibacterial film
Fabrication and antibacterial properties of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite and ACMCD-Ag/PMMA thin film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image) and (b) the particle size distribution of the CMCD.
The FTIR contrastive analysis of the obtained amphiphilic CMCDs (ACMCDs) and hydrophilic CMCDs (HCMCDs).
TEM images (inset: HRTEM images) of two fractions. (a) ACMCDs; (b) HCMCDs.
Comparison of UV–vis absorption spectra of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite and ACMCDs in solution.
The TEM and HRTEM images of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite.

Representative images of agar plates containing CMCD-Ag and AgNO3 impregnated disks and diameter of inhibition zone for (a) ACMCD-Ag, S. aureus, (b) AgNO3, S. aureus, (c) ACMCD-Ag, E. coli, and (d) AgNO3, E. coli.
The scanning electron microscope contrastive images of PMMA composite film, with different ACMCD-Ag composite doping amounts. (a) 0%, (b) 1%, (c) 2%, (d) 4%.
The UV–vis absorption spectra of these PMMA composite films with different ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite doping amounts.
Minimum inhibitory concentration (μgmL−1, calculated by Ag) of AgNO3 and ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite for two microorganisms.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: MICa, minimum inhibitory concentration (μgmL−1) of AgNO3, MICb, minimum inhibitory concentration (μgmL−1) of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial test results on S. aureus and E. coli of the novel CMCD-Ag/PMMA thin film (CAF: CMCD-Ag/PMMA antibacterial film).

head.ce:title: Application of cow milk-derived carbon dots/Ag NPs composite as the antibacterial agent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cow milk-derived carbon dots (CMCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of cow milk, and the as-prepared CMCDs were further extracted by ethyl acetate to obtain amphiphilic CMCDs (ACMCDs). Using the ACMCDs both as a reducing agent and a template, the ACMCDs-supported silver nanoparticles (ACMCD-Ag nanocomposites) were prepared, which showed good biocidal effect on both Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) bacterias. After that, a novel ACMCD-Ag/polymethylmethacrylate nanocomposite antibacterial film was fabricated by solvent casting method. Due to the excellent antibacterial, light admitting, and flexible properties, the nanocomposite antibacterial film is considered to be of great potential in applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As an alternative to traditional semiconducting quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention due to their fascinating attributes, such as photostability [1], low cytotoxicity [2,3], high biocompatibility, and electron transfer behavior [4,5]. However, many scientific issues with CDs still await further investigation, such as the accurate control of the resulting surface chemistry [6], the fabrication of CDs nanocomposite [7–10], moreover, the expansion of CDs for novel applications [11,12].

Recently, amphiphilic CDs which are compatible with organic solvents have attracted particular attention because of their great potential in fabricating composite materials and optoelectronic devices. To obtain CDs with amphiphilic performance, peach gum polysaccharide [13], cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride [14], and egg yolk [15] have been used as the carbon source based on the hydrothermal carbonization [13], plasma fabrication [15], and so on. However, the methods suitable to prepare amphibious CDs are still quite limited, which restricted the popularization and applications of the amphiphilic CDs to some extent. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop new approach to prepare amphiphilic CDs.

On the other hand, nature provides nearly boundless resource for scientists to get bio-inspiration for preparing nanomaterials with novel structures and properties. Several successful demonstrations were given to prepare CDs using natural biomass, such as bagasse [16], orange juice [17], and grass [18]. However, the as-prepared CDs are usually a mixture of carbon particles with different surface characteristics. Preparation of CDs with uniform surface properties is therefore important to understand the structure–activity relationship of CDs [17], which is also of great benefit to their subsequent applications [19]. So, it might be of great advantage and challenge to develop a simple separation method for accurately controlling the surface chemistry of CDs.

In the present work, cow milk-derived CDs (CMCDs) in aqueous solution were extracted by ethyl acetate to prepare amphiphilic CMCDs (ACMCDs), which might supply a simple and common strategy to obtain amphiphilic CDs. The ACMCDs were supported by silver nanoparticles using the ACMCDs both as a reducing agent and a template, then a novel ACMCD-Ag/polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) antibacterial film was fabricated by solvent casting method. Due to the excellent antibacterial, light admitting, and flexible properties, the nanocomposite antibacterial film is considered to be of great potential in applications, such as food packaging, drinking water disinfection, and sanitary appliance.

The cow milk was purchased from the Inner Mongolia Yili Industrial Group Co., Ltd. Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) and AgNO3 were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl acetate, N,N-dimethylformamide and other chemicals were all commercially available and used as received. Escherichia coli (ATCC 25,922) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25,923) were provided from China General Microbiological Culture Collection Center. Distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL-2010 TEM (Japan) at 200kV, which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The morphological structures of the antibacterial films were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a Hitachi SX-650 machine (Tokyo, Japan). Elemental and functional groups analyses were performed on Elementar Vario MICRO CUBE and FTIR-8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer.

CMCDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of cow milk. In a typical synthesis, cow milk (5mL) was diluted to be 30mL by adding deionized water, and the mixture was transferred to a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for a period of 12h. The CMCDs were then collected by removing the large dots though centrifugation at 11,000rpm for 5min, yielding a yellow solution (yield: ca. 34.5%). While the yield of the CMCD was calculated by the following equation:
Y =
W
acd
W
tcd
× 100 %
where Y is the yield of the CMCD, W acd is the active output of the CMCD (g), and W tcd is the theoretical output of the CMCD (g). The formation process of CMCD is considered to be the carbonization process of the constituents of cow milk, such as sugar, proteins, and lipids, similar to that in the formation of egg-derived CDs [15], so the dry weight of the cow milk (W dcm) was used as the theoretical output of CMCD [18].
The CMCDs solution thus obtained was diluted to 50mL with deionized water. After that, 25mL ethyl acetate was used to extract the CMCDs for three times to obtain ACMCDs (yield: 18%).

The ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite was prepared according to a previously reported method [20]. Typically, an ACMCD and AgNO3 blend solution (1:1, w/w) in ethanol was prepared at the concentration of 2mgmL−1. This solution was placed in a UV quartz cuvette (1cm×1cm×5cm) and exposed to UV light with wavelength of 254nm for 100min using a 6W UV lamp (Vilber Lourmat). The cell was kept 3cm apart from the light source. After the photoreaction, the color of solution changed from light yellow to dark brown, implying the formation of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the ACMCD-Ag/PMMA antibacterial film
ce:section.ce:para: PMMA powder (200mg) was dissolved in DMF (3mL), followed by the addition of the ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite (0mg, 2mg, 4mg, 8mg) which were dispersed in DMF (1mL). Then the mixture was ultrasound for 1h, and refluxed for 2 days. Subsequently, the hybrid material was casted onto clean quartz glasses. The novel ACMCD-Ag/PMMA antibacterial film was obtained after the total evaporation of solvent at room temperature.

E. coli and S. aureus were cultivated in sterilized Luria–Bertani broth and then incubated overnight at 37°C with a shaking incubator. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) test was performed according to a reported procedure, while the survival of organisms was observed by visual inspection. For the disk diffusion test, an equal amount of AgNO3 and ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite (100±10μg, calculated by Ag) were loaded onto the filter papers. The bacterial suspension (100μL of 104–105 CFUmL−1) was applied uniformly on the surface of the nutrient agar plates before placing the disks on the plates. The plates were incubated at 35°C for 24h, and the zones of inhibition were measured. Antibacterial test was performed by film attachment method, as reported previously [21].

The low TEM image and size distribution of the CMCDs thus formed are shown in Fig. 1 , which indicated that the CMCDs were well dispersed with a narrow size distribution (average diameter=1–5nm) and well separated from each other. High resolution TEM image reveals the lattice fringes of CMCDs to be 0.206nm (Fig. 1a), which agrees well with the 〈102〉 spacing of graphitic (sp 2) carbon. FTIR spectra of the CMCDs are shown in Fig. S1. The peak between 3200 and 3700cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching of OH and NH bonds [13], and the peaks at 2923 and 2850cm−1 are assigned to the stretching of CH bond [15]. The peak at 1420cm−1, 1570cm−1, 1635cm−1 can be identified as the stretching of CC group [13], the bending of NH bond, and the stretching of CO group respectively [22]. Due to the π–π* transition of CC bonds and n–π* transition of CO bonds, the UV–vis spectrum of the obtained CMCDs (Fig. S2) reveals a broad absorption band centered at 274nm [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and antibacterial properties of ACMCD-Ag nanocomposite and ACMCD-Ag/PMMA thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the nanocomposite also showed better biocidal effect on Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus than that of E. coli. In the case of Gram-positive bacteria, the enhanced antimicrobial activity of the ACMCD-Ag could be described by the cell structure. Gram-positive S. aureus has a loose cell wall, which could be readily attacked by nanoparticles. As a result, the released silver nanoparticles could pass through the S. aureus cell wall and bind with DNA, causing DNA denaturation more easily than that of E. coli [26].

Furthermore, the images of thin films as a function of doping level of ACMCD-Ag are shown in Fig. S4. As can been from the figure, the transparency of the thin films gradually decreases as the doping level increases, which is consistent with the SEM results. It could be seen from the photograph of the antibacterial film product (doping amount: 2%) as shown in Fig. S5 that the novel thin film had good flexibility and transparency, which is very important for thin films to apply in practical use.

Finally, the antibacterial property was evaluated by thin film attachment method, and the results showed that the percent reduction of bacteria was approximately 100% against both S. aureus and E. coli after 24h incubation (Table 2 ). Therefore, the ACMCD-Ag/PMMA thin film has a high antimicrobial efficiency regardless of Gram classes. To our best knowledge, this is the first time using amphiphilic CDs as the precursor to prepare antibacterial thin film, which is of great potential in many fields of chemical industry, such as food packaging, drinking water disinfection, and sanitary appliance [28].

In conclusion, cow milk-derived carbon dots (CMCDs) in aqueous solution were extracted by ethyl acetate to obtain ACMCDs. Using the ACMCDs both as a reducing agent and a template, the ACMCD-Ag nanocomposites were prepared, which showed good biocidal effect on both Gram-positive (S. Aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) strains. After that, a novel ACMCD-Ag/PMMA nanocomposite antibacterial film was fabricated by solvent casting method. Due to the excellent antibacterial, light admitting, and flexible properties, the nanocomposite antibacterial film is considered to be of great potential in applications. Our work is expected to pave a new way to study the surface chemistry and applications of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project 21471071, 21431002). The authors are indebted to Wenbing Zhang (Feifan photography shop, Lanzhou) for taking photos. They also wish to thank Xinghua Li (Northwest University, Xi’an) for carrying out all the TEM measurements, and Dr. Jiang Wu and Peng Wang for the supplement of lyophilizer.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2014.12.074.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are supplementary data to this article:
